macro_segments.title: The Basket Principle: Whether Binding in a Vessel Creates Unity (Egged Kli Shemi Egged)

micro_segments.title: Opening Discussion: The Simple Reading of Yesterday's Mishnah About Baskets and Fruit
Rava's Position: Distinction Between Large Fruits and Small Seeds (Gourds vs. Mustard)
Abaye's Opposing Position: Even Mustard Seeds Are Exempt When Basket Is Partially Inside
The Reversal Tradition: Rava and Abaye Switch Their Positions at Different Times
Tosafot's Resolution: The Later Statement Reflects Their Revised Positions
Testing the Reversal: Abaye and Rava's Statements About Hand vs. Vessel
Rashi's Clarification: The Apple in Hand Is Fully Outside; It's the Lack of Rest, Not Connection to Body, That Matters
Resolution: Basket as Single Unit vs. Apple as Distinct Object—Different Categories of Objects

macro_segments.title: The Mishnah on Carrying in Various Ways: Normal vs. Unusual Methods (Shinuy and Derech Hamotzi'in)

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah: Methods of Carrying in Hand, on Chest, on Shoulders—Based on Bnei Kehat
Rashi on Bnei Kehat: Why Enumerate Multiple Carrying Methods—Normal Ways vs. Special Cases
The Mishnah Continues: Unusual Methods—Back of Hand, Leg, Mouth, Elbow, Ear, Hair, Belt, Hem, Shoe, Sandal
Exemption for Unusual Carrying: Shlo Hotzi K'Derech Ha-Motzi'in—Not the Normal Way
The Concept of Shinuy (Unusual Way): Introduction and General Scope
Does Shinuy Affect the End Result? Does It Need to Be Harder? Does Shinuy Apply Outside Shabbat?
The Aglei Tal's View: Shinuy as Tied to Melechet Machshevet; Broader vs. Narrow Applications
Two Aspects of Shinuy: Universal Principle vs. Shabbat-Specific Exemption

macro_segments.title: How High Must One Carry? Above Ten Tefachim as the Measure (From Bnei Kehat in the Mishkan)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Elazar: Carrying Above Ten Tefachim Makes One Liable, Following the Practice of Bnei Kehat
How We Know Bnei Kehat Carried Above Ten Tefachim: The Altar and Mishkan Heights (Ten Amot)
Moshe and the Levites Were Ten Amot Tall: Derived from Spreading the Tent Cover
Alternative Proof from the Aron: Two-Thirds of Ten Tefachim Below the Pole, Making the Bottom Above Ten
Why Moshe Was Ten Amot: The Divine Presence Rests on the Tall and Wise (Chakham, Gibor, Ashir, U'Vaal Komah)

macro_segments.title: What Is Normal Carrying? Geographic and Universal Standards (Carrying on Head, Local Practices)

micro_segments.title: Rav Quoting Rabbi Chiya: Carrying on Head Is Liable If the People of Hutzal Do It That Way
The Gemara Objects: Should Hutzal's Practice, If Most of the World, Define the Universal Norm?
Revised Statement: In One's Own City, Following the Custom of That City; But Does Local Custom Override Universal?
Gemara's Strong Position: Local Custom Is Nullified; We Go by Universal Norms, Not Particular Regions
Tosafot's Complication: Other Sugyot Suggest Regional Norms Matter; Determining When Each Applies

macro_segments.title: Intent and Process: When You Intend One Thing but Another Happens (Mitkavein L'Fanav, Ba Lo L'Acharav)

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah: Intended in Front but Ended Up Behind—Exempt; Intended Behind but Ended Up in Front—Liable
Exception: The Woman with Apron—Normal for It to Reverse, So Both Front and Back Make Her Liable
Rabbi Yehuda: Same Applies to Receipt-Keepers and Officials Whose Belts Often Reverse Position
Gemara's Initial Problem: The Mishnah Seems to Make Sense; Why Would Anyone Find a Contradiction?
Rava's Explanation: Protection Level Differs—Lower Intent, Higher Result = Liable; Higher Intent, Lower Result = Exempt
Rav Elazar's Radical View: Two Separate Sources Contradicting Each Other; Debate on What's Normal
Rav Ashi's Resolution: The Mishnah Makes Perfect Sense; The Novelty Is Carrying Behind You Is Still Liable
Baraita on Money Belt: Opening Facing Up = Liable; Facing Down = Rabbi Yehuda Liable, Sages Exempt
The Real Debate: Which Category Does Belt-Facing-Down Fall Into? Normal (Like Carrying Behind) or Unusual (Like Back of Hand)?
Consensus Framework: Normal Is Not Narrow; It Includes Carrying Behind You—Some Things Are Borderline

macro_segments.title: Two People Carrying: Exemption When Either Could Do It Alone; Liability When Both Must

micro_segments.title: Simple Case: One Loaf—Liable; Two People Carry It—Exempt (Shnayim She'asu)
Exception: Sofa Requiring Two People—Both Liable When Two Carry It Normally
Rabbi Shimon's View: Always Exempt When Two People Do It; Melacha Requires One Person
Three-Way Debate: Rabbi Meir (Always Liable), Rabbi Yehuda (Exempt if One Could Do It; Liable if Both Must), Rabbi Shimon (Always Exempt)
Asymmetric Case: One Person Able, One Unable—Everyone Agrees the Able One Is Liable
Baraita Repeating the Positions: Loaf of Bread with Shnayim She'asu and the Three Opinions

topical_tags.term: Klacher Yad (Back of Hand—Exemplar of Unusual)
Ten Tefachim / Ten Amot Height Standard
Normal Range vs. Unusual: Geographic and Universal Standards
Rabbi Yehuda on Money Belt and Custom
Rabbi Meir vs. Rabbi Yehuda vs. Rabbi Shimon on Two People
